summary: A neuroscientist delivers a scathing critique of the EU's Human Brain Project, arguing that its goal of simulating a human brain is scientifically absurd. The author systematically dismantles the project's premises by pointing out that we cannot even simulate the 302-neuron brain of a roundworm despite having complete wiring diagrams and extensive data. The rant highlights fundamental gaps in neuroscience: we don't understand brain connectivity at fine scales, don't know what makes human brains special, can't agree on basic biophysical parameters, and don't even know what aspects of neural activity matter. The author compares the project's flashy simulations to cargo cult science—superficially similar to the real thing but lacking any understanding of underlying principles.
key_points: Despite complete connectome data for C. elegans (302 neurons), we still cannot simulate or understand how even this simple brain works, proving that data does not equal understanding
Brain connectivity is essentially unknown at fine scales—we have only millimeter-resolution maps, while the relevant circuitry operates at microscopic levels
Reported values for basic biophysical parameters vary wildly between studies, making accurate simulation impossible
Fundamental questions remain unanswered, like whether spike timing or spike rate carries information, even in the most-studied brain region (cat visual cortex)
The project's flashy visualizations are 'cargo cult science'—superficially similar to brains but lacking the principles that make them work
money_quotes: Simply put, data does not equal understanding.

We can't simulate the brain of C. Elegans, a very well studied roundworm in which every animal has exactly the same 302-neuron brain and we know the wiring diagram and we have tons of data on how the animal behaves.
One lengthy detailed study will find a parameter to be 35±4, and the next in-depth study will find the same parameter to be 12±3. So what should you use in your simulation?
The next time you see a pretty 3D picture of many neurons being simulated, think 'cargo cult brain'.
That simulation isn't gonna think any more than the cargo cult planes are gonna fly.
We can only create pretty things that are superficially similar in the ways that we currently understand, which an enlightened being would just laugh at.
In private the neuroscientists would say something more like 'hey, maybe it's crazy, but it'll bring a bunch of money.'
key_insight: Massive data collection and impressive visualizations cannot substitute for genuine scientific understanding of underlying principles—a lesson the billion-euro Human Brain Project exemplifies.
